original_hypothesis.title: LockingMultiRewards processExpiredLocks blocks processing lastLockIndex when other locks exist
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: processExpiredLocks explicitly prevents processing the lock at lastLockIndex when locks.length > 1 (line: `if (index == lastLockIndex[user] && locks.length > 1) revert ErrInvalidLockIndex()`). If a user's most recent lock expires before older locks, the operator cannot process it until all other locks are also processed or expired. This creates a lock ordering dependency that can trap expired funds.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The swap-and-pop mechanism requires the most recently created lock to be processed last to maintain correct lastLockIndex tracking. But this creates scenarios where expired funds remain locked simply due to processing order constraints.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (index == lastLockIndex[user] && locks.length > 1) revert ErrInvalidLockIndex(); blocks processing most recent lock when others exist

preconditions: The most recently created lock (at lastLockIndex) has expired
Other locks at different indices have NOT expired

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Explicit revert when processing lastLockIndex with locks.length > 1
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Expired funds at lastLockIndex are trapped until all other locks can be processed
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No alternative mechanism for users to claim expired locks directly

analysis_questions: Under what realistic scenarios does the most recent lock expire before older locks?
How long can funds be trapped in the worst case?
Can the operator work around this by processing in a specific order?
validation_requirements: Create a user with multiple locks where lastLockIndex expires first
Attempt to process the expired lastLockIndex lock
Verify it reverts and funds remain locked

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: User creates locks over different epochs. Due to the epoch-aligned unlock times and the swap-and-pop reordering from previous processExpiredLocks calls, the lock at lastLockIndex may have an earlier unlockTime than other locks. When this lock expires, the operator cannot process it because the guard prevents it. The user's expired tokens remain locked until other locks expire, potentially weeks later.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary fund lock beyond expected unlock time; no permanent loss
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Users with multiple locks whose most recent lock expires first
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Funds are released when other locks expire and can be processed

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for processing lastLockIndex when other locks haven't expired

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable by checking lock states on-chain

validation_result.impact_summary: The described scenario is impossible because the lock at lastLockIndex always has the latest or equal unlockTime, so it cannot expire before other locks.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis claims that processExpiredLocks blocks processing of the lastLockIndex lock when other locks exist, trapping expired funds if the most recent lock expires before older locks. However, this precondition is provably impossible.

In _createLock (LockingMultiRewards.sol), new locks are always pushed to the end of the array with unlockTime = nextUnlockTime() = nextEpoch() + lockDuration. Since nextEpoch() monotonically increases over time (it returns the current epoch boundary + rewardsDuration), locks created in later epochs always have later unlockTimes. Locks created in the same epoch are merged into the existing lock at lastLockIndex rather than creating new entries.

Therefore, lastLockIndex always points to the most recently created lock which has the latest (or equal) unlockTime. The swap-and-pop mechanism in processExpiredLocks correctly tracks lastLockIndex through array mutations via the guard: `if (lastLockIndex[user] == lastIndex) { lastLockIndex[user] = index; }`. This ensures lastLockIndex follows the most recently created lock regardless of array reordering.

Since the lock at lastLockIndex always has the latest unlockTime, by the time it expires, all other locks must have also expired. The operator can process the earlier-expiring locks first (which is required by the guard) and then process the lastLockIndex lock when it's the only remaining one. No expired funds are ever trapped.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The guard at line 410-412 is correct and does not cause fund trapping. The lock at lastLockIndex always has >= unlockTime compared to all other locks, making the precondition of the hypothesis impossible.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This function shows that new locks are always created with the LATEST unlockTime (nextUnlockTime()), and lastLockIndex is set to the newly pushed lock's index. Locks in the same epoch are merged. This guarantees lastLockIndex always points to the lock with the latest unlockTime.

The guard prevents processing lastLockIndex when other locks exist, and the swap-and-pop logic correctly updates lastLockIndex when the tracked lock moves. Since lastLockIndex always has the latest unlockTime, by the time it expires, all other locks have also expired and can be processed first.

calling_sequence: 1. _createLock sets lastLockIndex to newly pushed lock index, which has unlockTime = nextUnlockTime()
2. nextUnlockTime() = nextEpoch() + lockDuration, where nextEpoch() increases monotonically
3. Locks from same epoch are merged (else branch), locks from later epochs are pushed with later unlockTime
4. processExpiredLocks swap-and-pop correctly tracks lastLockIndex via: if (lastLockIndex[user] == lastIndex) lastLockIndex[user] = index
5. The lock at lastLockIndex always has >= unlockTime compared to all other locks
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The precondition 'lastLockIndex lock expires before others' is impossible. In _createLock, new locks are pushed with unlockTime = nextEpoch() + lockDuration. Since nextEpoch() monotonically increases, newer locks always have later or equal unlockTimes. lastLockIndex tracks the newest lock through swap-and-pop operations. Therefore, by the time the lastLockIndex lock expires, all other locks with earlier unlockTimes have already expired and can be processed first.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: LockingMultiRewards.s.sol deploys with 7 days rewardsDuration and 13 weeks lockDuration. maxLocks = 13 weeks / 7 days = 13. This means locks from different weeks have different unlockTimes, with later-created locks always having later unlockTimes.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario states: 'User creates locks over different epochs. Due to the epoch-aligned unlock times and the swap-and-pop reordering from previous processExpiredLocks calls, the lock at lastLockIndex may have an earlier unlockTime than other locks.' This is incorrect. The swap-and-pop reordering in processExpiredLocks correctly tracks lastLockIndex through array mutations. The lock at lastLockIndex always has the latest unlockTime because: (1) it was the most recently created lock, (2) newer locks always have later or equal unlockTimes, and (3) the tracking update `if (lastLockIndex[user] == lastIndex) { lastLockIndex[user] = index; }` correctly follows the lock through array reordering. Extensive tracing through multiple scenarios confirms the lastLockIndex invariant is maintained.
validation_result.impact_assessment: No impact. The described scenario cannot occur. The guard is a correct design choice that enforces proper processing order for the swap-and-pop mechanism. The lock at lastLockIndex always expires last (or simultaneously with other locks from the same epoch), so requiring it to be processed last does not trap any expired funds.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockingMultiRewards with 7-day rewardsDuration, 13-week lockDuration. 2. Create locks for a user across 3 different epochs (weeks). 3. Verify that lastLockIndex always points to the newest lock. 4. Advance time past the first lock's expiry. 5. Attempt to process lastLockIndex - verify it reverts. 6. Process the first lock. 7. Verify lastLockIndex is correctly updated. 8. Advance time past all locks' expiry. 9. Process locks in order (non-lastLockIndex first, then lastLockIndex last). 10. Verify all funds are correctly unlocked.
uncertainties: The analysis assumes no external mechanism can modify _userLocks or lastLockIndex outside of _createLock and processExpiredLocks - verified by code inspection that these are the only write paths.
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test: create random lock patterns across epochs, process expired locks in various orders, verify lastLockIndex always points to the lock with the latest unlockTime
Invariant test: after any sequence of _createLock and processExpiredLocks calls, the lock at lastLockIndex has unlockTime >= all other locks' unlockTimes
